transcript.transcript: Hi, my name is Thad Webster and I'm a participant in the American 250 in Idaho Applied Oral History Project. Today is Wednesday, March 25th, 2026, and I'm at the Idaho State Historical Society Archives with Ramona and Adelia Garro to talk about their memories of the Boise Depot and trains. This oral history is conducted with the support of the Idaho State Historical Society and software company, TheirStory, as a part of the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. The A250 Oral History Project supports the commemoration's mission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution, equality, liberty and justice, through meaningful community engagement, and to recognize, appreciate and commemorate Idaho's journey in America's legacy. Ramona and Adelia, off camera for a minute. first, let me say thank you so much for your time today. This interview will allow you to reflect on and share your memories and experiences about trains in Boise. Through these questions, I hope to gain insights into Boise citizens' experiences and memories with Boise Train Services. I'll start the interview, the general interview, with some questions, and that'll just lead to conversation that we'll have. So before we jump into the questions, I have these questions for you. Now we have the pause. It's paused, I think. And you can come around here. I'll sit there. And make sure if you're together. Make sure the camera's on you all. Okay. And then I will click this and we'll see if it works. Okay. There you are. I'm going to look up here to see if something a little red button starts up there. Well, it's just a white light. Now here's the question to start with. It's very easy. Let's do it up straight. Can you give me permission to record this interview? Yes. Yes we do. Good. And can I have you each state and spell your first name and last name? Do I go first? Ramona Higer, r a m o n a h I g e r. Adelia Simplot, a d e l I a s i m p l o t. Thank you very much. And we're underway. Here's the first question to get us going. I'd like for each of you to share your memory of the first time you remember seeing the Boise Depot. And tell me about it. What's your first memory? Well, when you went with our aunt. Right now, where you got three. Well, the war. So I must have been five. And my aunt came out to visit us from Colorado, from Colorado. And so we went to take her to be on the train to go home. And when we walked in it, it was almost like a beehive. It was all soldiers everywhere, some their duffel bags were on the on the floor. Yeah. It was this beautiful old depot. It was a big building to me. And but it was like a beehive of, of soldiers and it was. It was just an amazing sight to me. Interesting. Five years. Old. Yeah. Yeah, that's a great memory. Were they serving refreshments? No. They were going to get on the train and go to war. That's true. First for you? You know, I it's a funny thing. It was just always there. And it was always so beautiful that I grew to, to to just love it. And then when anyone tried to do anything to it, we would all rise up. Yeah. We didn't even like to have it repainted, but, so I don't know, I must have been 5 or 6 because we were attracted to the Platt Gardens. Oh, yes. On a hot day, if we were going somewhere. We didn't live near there, but sometimes you'd be there. And there was a darling little gazebo house off to the side that gets torn down every 25 years and reinterpreted. Yeah. But, it was, do you remember that? It was very fancy. It was off to the left. I do. Mhm. So that that was kind of my first. Yeah. So where were you living when you were growing up and would come over to the depot? We lived on a small farm over by, um. We lived on Iowa Street, Iowa, Iowa. And, we had, my mother when she married my father, they lived on Bannock. Mhm. And, she said she wanted a farm. So my father being a very shrewd Basque. Mhm, he talked to the banker whose hair my father cut and said he wanted to buy property. My. And the old banker told him to go out,and this property was had been, not paid taxes. Oh, okay. Foreclosed or whatever. Yeah. So there was a great parcel of land. 35 acres and the beautiful old stone house on Broadway. My mother loved that. She wanted it, but my father, being a practical man, said, no, I can't take care of my business, and 35 acres also. So he bought five acres and had our home built. Oh. And, that home was built in 1935 and only two families have ever lived in it. Our family. And then when our mother passed away, we sold it. And that family has been there ever since. Oh. And the cute story is that the guy on the basement floor is her hopscotch that she drew with chalk. He said, don't, don't cover that up. Don't cover that. Up. I forgot that. Oh, so that was, that was like the South Boise Village area and it may have. Iowa may have even been a little bit outside of what was originally the village. It was. Area, as I recall. Eight blocks or more. Yeah, I don't remember calling it a village though. Right? No they didn't. Not not by not by the war. In fact, I think I mean, it was a village at the turn of the century. It was incorporated as a as a town. And then it was. And then it was incorporated. Into Boise in 1913. Okay. Well, before you were born stuff. But at that time it was this separate, incorporated village. And that's where the streetcar went out. Oh, it went that far. It went out to Ivy. Ivy. Ivy. Ivy. Crossed Broadway Bridge all the way out to Ivy Wild. And then it came back. But but the streetcars, you know, stopped. Yeah. Before you were born. Yeah. I remember. I remember somehow seeing those. Some of those tracks. They were still there when we were little. Yeah. And we like the architecture. You know, there's the Dutch house, right? Where kind of what you'd say is the center of the. Of that little town. And then the Mexican Spanish was there, and then Toby's, electrical or whatever. You remember Mr. Thompson? Well, Thompson's. Appliance, is it called appliance? Yeah. Oh, and dad called him old Mr. Thompson. Toots, toots, Toots. Thompson. So that would be Broadway Street. And those buildings are still there. It's kind of the IGA building. It's an old Dutch kind of looking building that would have been from the 1800s. Was your dad's barbershop? Yes. On Broadway. Is that still there? The building is still his. His first barbershop was across the street where the there's a restaurant there now. Cafe Rey Pratt owned those buildings. And then, um somehow, Mr. Anderson owned the building that looked very Spanish. And our father then moved over there and rented from Mr. C at Andy Anderson. Oh. Okay. So if you would go down Broadway towards town, then the latest depot, because Boise had three train depots, the one up on the hill would have been off to the left as you're going down Broadway, right? You could hit Boise Avenue on Broadway, and then you'd follow Boise Avenue around and you'd be underneath the depot, right? So to speak. Or you could you could go, and I'm not sure at that time what the streets were and stuff, but you could come up like the highway, highway 30. I think it was became Federal way Highway. If you got over to that you could come up to the depot. You could that way. So when I was in the fourth grade, Mr. Lynch, our principal, had us go out and, uh, pound this wooden thing into the ground because that was the Oregon Trail. Oh, that's the Oregon Trail. Right on. Boise Avenue. The Oregon Trail, and it's still there. When I drive by Garfield, I look in the old wooden post is. It was it was. It must have been an anniversary of the Oregon Trail. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Anyway, so, so you didn't remember then any family trips over to the depot to pick up someone or to go? Did you all did you ever leave on the train from the Boise depot? Either of you. Did. Aunt Ida, she came. Well, that's who we took that time. Her husband worked on the railroad, so she would come out on the train and go on the train. Yeah. And that she was in Colorado? Yeah. And so you rode the train from the depot back there once. I don't remember ever riding it. Well, I rode the train to Portland with my uncle, my mother's brother, when I was in the fifth grade. Okay. And, um, he was going home. He. I don't know where he'd been. Anyway, he, he stopped at our home and, he was going to Portland. His family was there and I went with him. You know me to visit? Yeah. Just have the suitcase. will travel. So off we went and on the train, you know. I mean, I'm sure people wonder what this old this man was doing, this little girl. I was having a wonderful time with. My. I wish my husband were here. He. when he'd go to college, he would. In the east to Harvard. Oh, okay. He, his father was an attorney, and he represented, um, Union Pacific. So Dale went as far as as as that was Union Pacific. Yeah. And then he had. Switched to another train. Yeah. Yeah. But I don't think I one time they brought the train back probably the last within the last 20 years and everybody went to see it. That was probably the only time I was ever in the engine. Yeah. Oh well the what. The train. It came through in the middle of the night and we would hear it. Yeah. Because as it would get up, we lived down here and it's up there and and it was very quiet in those days. Farmland. Yeah. But then that old train would blow his whistle letting people know he was coming. You could hear it. Yeah. Mother was a grandma or Uncle Jesse's uncle Jesse's train. We all love it. Love that whistle. We did. Now, you said your, your father's brother going back to Portland. I remember reading a story that you told about him going to barber school in Portland. He did? With the with. With his brother. His brother, and maybe with some other guys from here. Yes. Yeah. To learn how to be a barber. Right? He was tired of sheep herding. Yeah, well, that's a hard life. Yes. Really hard. Yeah. He was a good barber. My dad. When he was herding sheep, he came across this fish, a salmon, but he didn't know what it was. So he had his rifle and he shot it. He'd never seen anything like that. Oh, wow. Nor do we now see anything that gets back this far. Yeah. So, our father, he didn't talk too much about, when he was a herder, but it was a hard life and, lonely. And, so my son, when he was about 16, said he was tired of the world and he was going to go off into I to get away from civilization. And my father said, civilization looks pretty good when you've been out on the sheep [grounds] for six months. Yeah, yeah, we need to write that down. I never I That made me laugh. That's the truth. Looked pretty good. Yeah. Do you all know where you were around Thanksgiving, 1951. November 25th 1951. near Thanksgiving. Well, we would have been at our home, and mother always had a Thanksgiving dinner. Mhm. Do you remember on that day hearing the train crash? Oh, out at Orchard? No. That would have been too far away from us. I've never heard of it. Rosalie [Bell - a friend of Ramona] heard of it? She did? She heard it. Oh my God. Oh my goodness. At her father's house on Overland and Robert. Well, the the one thing I remember about that was said to me about the train. My mother had a friend called Miss Ruth Redmond. Ruth Redmond. And, she was a spinster. And, she would come to visit my mother, and she had a funny little old car because I was just a little girl. So it was probably 1939, probably before the war, because obviously she had gasoline to get around. Yeah. And, I remember, you know, mother waving goodbye to her and then she goes off. Well, it wasn't much longer than, that, that she was crossing the train track up on the bench and she was killed. Oh, Miss Ruth Redmond. Wow. And so that would be in the. Yeah. Yeah. I mean, not very many people got killed by trains, but her car stalled. Yeah. You know, and she was a spinster lady, and, she probably didn't know how to get the darn thing started. Yeah. And the train hit her. Yeah. And, that made me very sad. Yes. Yeah. Boy. That would. Ruth Redmond was her name. That that was. When, whenever, you had the chance to go by there as kids. The depot say, in the 50s, did you remember a time when, the depot was going through, reconstruction? Not until the Agees came. Do I recall. I kind of vaguely remember a little bit, but not. I don't remember a lot. But, yeah, when Bill Agee came in, he somehow wanted to put his name on it, you know? And so he put. They did it beautifully, though. They did. We were lucky in a way. Oh, yeah. So refreshed. Yeah. And I remember also that the, the, the, folklore folklore was that, young men coming through on troop trains. They looked up, they saw this beautiful, at the depot. You could see all the way down to the Capitol building. And several of them said they were coming back there when they got home, and they did. Oh. I that's what I've been told. I had an addendum because, Mr. Angleton, who was a do you remember him? He was a famous merchant here in Boise. I do know something about. So his brother was, it was it was wartime and they were coming and all the everything was darkened down on the train so no one could look out. But he had a little area where he could look out and he saw the depot and he said, well, I got here in Boise. They had started in California, I think. Oh, they were moving them across. They were moving them. And he was with the CIA. He was one of the very early. So it might have been a special group of men too. He was with the CIA. Yeah. Yeah. It's interesting. I remember the Angleton store. I got here in 79, worked with HP. And my wife and I had just come back from Germany. Had a two year assignment there in Germany. So I turned that and we had just been full of living there, driving around, you know, seeing places, having our eyes open and coming back and that was in 83. And then we're downtown. Oh. And we we bumped, well, we bumped into this Angleton store. Oh, okay. And we walk in there and we go, this is just what we left because, Angleton imported all of this European. He did. Stuff. He did. And then we. I can remember we go in there because one thing we we bought some crystal over there and we couldn't find it anywhere back here. And we had broken one glass. We wanted to replace it. And they happen to have that kind. And every time we'd go in there either together or separate, there was never anyone in the store. Oh, really? In fact, it was hard, that hard to get someone to wait on us. Really? And we thought, well, this is the oddest high end product store in the middle of downtown. And, so anyway, I remembered the story. I was with a group recently, maybe 20, not 20, maybe 15 girls. Not one of them knew about Angleton. Really? I was so surprised that he was wiped off of the memory so quickly. Yeah. Oh, well, he and his brother was from Rome, wasn't he? Mr.. Mr.. was that his brother? And he said so some of his sister, His sister was they were his sister lived in Rome. Beautiful things to buy. And, his his brother was the head of the CIA. Yeah. in Washington. And, James Jesus Angleton and, Mr. Angleton's mother. She was in a convent in Mexico. She's Mexican, and he was one of the Rough Riders. You know the. well, not Teddy Roosevelt, but there was another, they had the, Geronimo. I mean. It was something. Something with a famous name, wasn't it, Pancho Villa? Well, it was during that time, and our troops went down there, and that's where Mr. Angleton, who was in the. He was in the cavalry, saw her and fell in love with her and married her. She was a Mexican. She was Mexican. Wow. And, so, you know, and she was in the convent. She was very proper. So he married her. And then they had two daughters and two sons. And the one was head of CIA and the other, poor Mr. Angleton had polio as a child, so he was kind of crippled. Oh, and, uh, that's kind of colored his life. Yeah. Yeah. And, and the and the sisters lived in Rome and in, um. Florence. Yeah. Yeah. So you don't remember going to pick anyone up, but you remember a story of someone who came through that you knew and picked up that story from? Yes. That's cool. I think I heard it from Mr. Angleton. I'm really quite, quite sure I did. I remember that our brother had a, they used to have races from the depot down, the boys. The boys would make these little race cars. Soapbox derby. Yeah. And our brother was in in it. Oh, I don't think he won but, I was just a little girl. So you mentioned that you were aware of the, Mr. Agee [MK CEO] buying the depot. that was in 1990, and then there was a couple of years for them to get in and restore and refurbish because nothing had really been done to the depot since 1925 when it opened up. And then they opened it, you know, with a great celebration or whatever. Did either of you attend the re-opening of the depot, or was there any events afterwards? Everybody was. There. It was such a thrilling time. And then they kind of changed some things. You know, they always, every few decades they do do that and move all the pews or move them all back or I know they changed the girl's room from the other way. And condiments. They used to have all the candies, remember? And the and the newsstand. You could buy newspapers and magazines and candy. Uh. it was beautiful. I don't know how they ever chose something so beautiful that, when they decided to do the trains, that they're always in every town. They're the most beautiful building. I mean. When I was going to ask you, why do you think the Union Pacific built such a beautiful, large, larger than was needed depot on a hill in a residential area? Yes. Was it? It's like it's like Disneyland. I mean, I can just imagine, you know, if you're on a train coming from the south into Boise. Yeah. And you're, you're seeing this view down the valley, open up, and then you turn and here's this depot. Yes, I would think I'm at Disneyland. This is like a Disneyland right here with a castle. It's interesting. It really was a beautiful depot because you've seen the depots in Nampa and Pocatello. They're awful. I mean, they have no style. Well, they have the style of the, of the, of the, of the era in which they were built. But this was a Spanish style. Yes. And I wonder if it was because, you know, they had Spanish village here and it didn't coincide with Harriman did it? Was Harriman choosing his ski property in the late 20s. 1935. So they didn't know that? Yeah. In fact, there wasn't, E.H. Harriman, Averell's father, the original chairman of the board of the Union Pacific, died before this was built. Okay. And Averell wasn't in the business until after it opened, like in the 30s. And then, of course, Averell went to work for the government during the war. Right. And and wasn't around. To Russia and everything. After that. Uh, so it was other people. There were other people that made the decision. Well, and, you know, also they built Sun Valley. Mhm. Yeah. Averell [Harriman]. Averell. And my father in law and mother in law [J. R, & Ruby Simplot], went there skiing when they were when they just opened up. I mean, they have movies of them, old movies that are kind of funny, you know? I think the Harrimans built that. Sun Valley. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. But Ruby and Jack were, they lived in Burley. They must have thought they'd died and gone to heaven. Sun Valley. With the movie stars. Yeah, with the movie stars. They had they skied here at Bogus too [Bogus Basin ski area, Boise]? Well. Or was that like. Well, J.R. Bogus he loved Bogus, but, she she gave up skiing long, long ago. They're both gone. But, when they were young, they lived in Burley, and so that was a big deal. I mean, hardly anybody from Burley would have ever taken up skiing, but they, that tells you that they were young people on the move. Yeah. It does. I mean, he wanted to be somebody. Yeah. Yeah. Well, that was quite a move forUnion Pacific and, and for Averell, although the interesting thing of it was it was built in the middle of the depression. Right. That's right. Sounds a bit ironic to be spending so much money, but train traffic dropped like a rock after the crash on Wall Street in 29. And so all of the train companies were looking for a way to get some people back on the train. That's right. That's right. And, and Averell had this idea of in Idaho, it had to be on his line. right. on our line, we're going to build, this the first destination ski resort in America. But at the same time, other competing train lines were building stations or little routes to national parks. Uh. Again, as a way to get people, people travel, people from the east, basically people with money. Yeah. To come out. And, so that's, there was a lot of interesting construction and kind of new developments that the train companies undertook that. Another big development that Averell Harriman was involved in, early in the 30s was the Union Pacific introducing what was called a Streamliner train. And this was a lightweight, instead of being steam, it was diesel powered. So they were, they were very innovative, and they had the money to, to do it. They were some of the largest corporations. First chairlift ever. They, they. Right. They were very innovative. So it's interesting, it really does pose questions. Yeah. Yeah. That's true. Um, yeah. So. Then the depot after it's opened 1993. Great fanfare. MK goes bankrupt. And they end up selling it to the city of Boise. What did you think of that? I was relieved. I was relieved. I mean, they had done the work, and it was. Couldn't have been more beautiful. The restoration. Yeah. It was like. And that was great. Luckily for us, Bill had been raised here. Bill Agee. Right. And so he remembered it as a beautiful building. And and I'm glad that he did that. Yeah. We can thank him for that. It's true. Yeah. So then the city took over, and, you'll have to excuse me if I sound like a tour guide, but this is what I did for seven years at the depot. I was a tour guide, so I would go through this story over and over again. What happened to. You? Were you were a docent or what did they call you? A manager, I think. Oh, a manager. I worked for the Parks and Rec department. Oh, wonderful. And I wasn't quite managing anything, but I was paid. And leading tours. So I got to know the depot and train history quite well in detail. So the city buys the depot. And all this while since Union Pacific stopped their trains, do you remember that June of 1971? The Portland Rose left town not to come back. It's right. All passenger trains stopped in June of 1971 because the government. Richard Nixon, President, had signed a law that was now moving responsibility for all public train travel to the government. Oh my gosh. And it became Amtrak. Yeah. But it didn't open up here for six years. So in 71, no more trains were going by. I guess to to 77. But Union Pacific was still here and owned the depot without any train business. Interesting. They must have had some maybe while you were up there. They had an old train that was going to go from Boise to Glenns Ferry, and maybe the old. Steam. Steam. And my mother in law, she was raised in Glenns Ferry. She took Ted and Lori [Adelia's children]. They were little kids and went on the train to Glenns Ferry and I, I don't I don't know, I didn't know what it was about, but it was something to do with, maybe. The last trip. The last train on that. That could be. You know, 71. It would have been okay. Now, after that, there. there have been periodically special trains, excursions across the country or. train events here or somewhere else with the train coming here and going. But there was no regularly scheduled passenger train after June of 71. And then Amtrak started up the route from Salt Lake to Portland in 77. While the Union Pacific still owned the train depot. They just sat there. Did Union Pacific sell Sun Valley then? Yes. Mr.. Jan's bill. Jan's yes. They did. But. But it was. Later. Yeah. Yeah. It wasn't connected to the train service, so to speak. Well, it was funny from the beginning that Canyon County won out to build and then ours was just a spur, wasn't it? Yeah. Would you call it a spur or something? So that's a good name for it. And then to make it so beautiful and proportioned. Well, that is funny because it had to be politics. Yeah yeah yeah. Especially never getting. Everything's always politics. Well, I do know that that that was kind of a political speech that the chairman of the Union Pacific made on April 16th, 1925, when he came here to open the depot. Oh. Because he said, we built this terminal for the State of Idaho for the City of Boise to have a better working relationship. Oh my. So there's a lot underneath. Also, of course, answered the question that everyone must have had. So when you were building this, did you actually intentionally decide to line it up with the Idaho State Capitol? And he didn't have to answer the question. He just said, we built this for a better relationship. Perfect. Yeah. Because it absolutely lines straight up. Um, they made it that way. Yeah. And I was so glad in the 60s, they, tried to put in some laws that, they, they couldn't have all this gaudy stuff. On the Building Capitol Boulevard, You know, they've kind of gotten away from it a little bit, but. Oh, they've completely gotten away from it. But I want to add this. Dale [Higer, Ramona's deceased husband] was on Planning and Zoning at that time. He made the motion that they had to stay back up to the original plan and he couldn't get a second. Oh, and, and then they did the Idaho First Bank and they just started encroaching old. Joe Bianco, I thought it was. Yeah. It was. Anyway, Dale tried. He tried. Yeah, yeah. Yeah. You always kind of feel like there's a little. Well, the other thing that was interesting about building that out there, of course, they put in a new road up to the depot and landscaped gardens, all of that stuff. Right? But there was no bridge over the river. There was no Capital Boulevard in 1925. Really. It was Seventh Street. The Capital started [at] Seventh Street, came down to the old Davis place at. the [Boise] River, stopped. The only bridge across the river there was Ninth Street Bridge. Which is still there. Just, Yeah, just west of. Amazing. And it wasn't until 1933 that the city finally agreed to build a bridge. Beautiful bridge. Beautiful. And tie the road going up to the capital with. Or to the train depot with the road going to the. And so what year was that? 29? 33 33 wow. Depression time. The C.C.C. boys were here. Yeah. Did the work. Yeah, that was one reason to get it done. So. Interesting. Amtrak started. None of you have ridden Amtrak. In the East, but not in the West. Not from here. Not to hear anything like that. The point I was getting on there with the ownership of the depot. Union Pacific build it, owned it, held on to it 71 after no trains were there. 77 Amtrak came there. The government contract with all the private railroad companies was $1 a year. Oh, interesting. To use any depots on passenger tracks, $1 a year to use any tracks where former passengers, $1 a year to use all passenger railway equipment. So Union Pacific had a contract to pay a dollar to the government? Heck of a deal. Yeah, yeah. Or they, they had a, a contract that Amtrak, the government would pay them $1. Right? And that lasted three years. Amtrak in here running the service before MK came in and said, we'll buy it from you. So then they buy it and they wanted to kick Amtrak out of the depot. Do you remember that? No, but it makes sense. I remember the people writing in and saying they had to have it. Exactly. I remember that. It was a big. Yeah, it was a big deal. Deal in the paper. I mean, the statesman that spring in 91 was just amazing. The City Council rejected the first two plans from MK that had the changes. It had Amtrak first as a little, they called it Amshack, little building down the track from the depot and so on and so forth. But of course, all of the people in the public said, what are you doing to our. That's right. Depo. Our depo. We always felt with our. We the people felt like it was theirs. Yeah, I mean. It wasn't, but they felt like it. MK had just paid $2 million for it. But it was our depo. What are you doing with our. So they had a big. And what they ended up doing was they finally got the answer right. They had their architects refurbish the east end of the depot, which had been a separate business. It was from 1925 until 1971 that was the Express business. The express business always worked hand in hand with the railroad business. Everywhere. Was that where the old wagons were? Yes. Yeah, I remember the wagons outside. Green. Yeah. Yes. Yes. And it was called R-E-A, Railway Express Agency. Okay. And it was a separate business. Okay. And when they made that building, they had a solid concrete wall down at the end between the baggage area and this other business. Just in case something went wild or what? To. well, to keep the businesses separate. Separate. So there'd be no collusion. No [hanky panky] No allegations. Yeah. Yeah. And no, no bags would be missing. And so what worked well for Amtrak and for MK was that the architects finally said well you've got a separate part of the building down there. It'll be separate from you and your whatever you want to do with the depot, we'll put the Amtrak station in there. And that's that's where the Amtrak. And that's how that station was. Yeah. Makes me want to go out and get on the train. I wish. I wish we had one to go. Thinking about a little story. You know how there was a little over thing? And then you come up and, and it was two lanes. [Vista Ave overpass] Do you remember it? It was off to the east side of the Vista. Anyway, I knew these two guys. One was kind of a boyfriend. He came to a party and he had just. He was going down, and for the hell of it, he went over in the other lane and the other. And then the other guy, this was what was so funny, he switched. He was the one driving the other car. And so I mean, they just went like that. They just it could have been like Ruth, like your best friend. That could have been worse. Oh, I think it came within an inch. And they were both so heightened when they told me at different times in one day. Isn't that something? Oh, crazy people will do. Our brother did a lot of crazy things. Yeah, yeah he did. Our brother had nine cat lines. And then more and more. So, then MK goes bankrupt. Yeah. Everywhere. Everywhere. The depot had nothing to do with it, but they went bankrupt everywhere. And of course, they had to sell this. And the city of Boise comes in to buy it, and they buy it in 1996. Okay. Amtrak still running. So Amtrak had the Union Pacific owning the depot that they worked in for part of the time. Then they had Morrison Knudsen owning the depot that they worked in, and they hung out on Amtrak, you know, shingle, little sign in the depot, and then the city of Boise for a year because Amtrak stopped in 1997. So does. Do you have to go to Salt Lake to get Amtrak now? Yeah. That's what people do, isn't. It, to go east? Yeah, to go east. You can't go west? No. Well, unless you go down south like LA. I think they may have their LA line still going. I remember the day that they fired Bill Agee. Yeah. I was at the hairdresser's, on Main Street, you know. Oh yeah. Graber's And the woman came in, she said they're dancing in the street. I think people are dancing in the street. That's really funny. We couldn't believe it. She said they fired Bill Agee. Oh. What a wretched man, But he did that good. He had a good investment. He was a pill. He was a pill. He. And he was a Boise boy. He went to Boise High with you. That's right. With you? No, he was a little older. Oh. He was a little older. But but he was a brave. Yeah, he was a. He was a red brave. But we thank you for saving the depot. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. And so, I mean, you know, there are things that people do that we're grateful for. Yeah. For sure. What do you think? And this could be the last, you know, topic unless you, you know, other things, In today, today's economy here in Treasure Valley and you all obviously are very fond of where you live. Treasure Valley. I chose to move here. I love it. I wouldn't go anywhere else. With the with a fondness for the Valley, how do you all feel about this nagging problem that everyone complains about at least every four years of traffic building up on the Interstate, when we have a railroad track running right beside it? What do you think? What's wrong? How come? How come it doesn't get worked out? I think it's odd myself because a lot of those people would go if there was parking at the depot. They would take that train home. I mean, I guess they would leave their cars in, in Caldwell, Nampa. And because that freeway is just it's just jammed. Yeah, it's jammed in the morning and it's jammed at night. And, um, it is odd, isn't it? Dale tried to do something about it and it just never could. He always envisioned, you know, us connecting with the Canyon County. But then he really worked hard to and in town to have. Some some kind of transit system. I can't remember which mayor that was. It was that I can't remember which mayor. But they worked hard and saw other towns. Do you remember? I'm not sure exactly when it was, but, oh, it had to be Mayor Cole's. Yeah. He was Mayor when the city bought the depot? No. Do you remember when he got in a demonstration train? Like a commuter train system for for citizens to ride on from Boise to Caldwell? Caldwell. I vaguely remember it. I know, vague, but. It was called the, like, sprinter or something was a German made, you know, commuter train. That's right, that's right. And the statesman. published, they, they essentially were promoting this like one two week trial in October. Mhm. Every day in the statesman of that year. Every day I could go in to Newspapers.com and I can type in like sprinter or something like that for that year, like I don't know, 80 something. And it'll give me 1400 responses because it's all the same ad [advertisement]. "Get ready to ride the whatever when it comes". What do you think kept it from coming? I don't know. I mean, that that's one of the questions that I have in my mind. Learning about trains in the Valley and all that kind of stuff is why. I think the traffic wasn't bad enough for people to be before, to take advantage of that they would now, if I mean, all you have to do is go on online at night, on, on TV and watch those cars. They're just it's just one massive. We were in Eagle [ID] yesterday, about 4:30. We were on our way home and all you could see were cars, cars lined up, and it wasn't even 5:00. And I said, these poor devils have to do this every day. They have to not only drive in, they have to drive back out. It's unbelievable. Yeah. It was unbelievable. Well, a lot of other cities have found a way. It's not all perfect or whatever, but they they have found a way to have an alternative. And I, I just don't know when Boise's going to. I think ours is relying on a bicycle now. The electric bicycle? Yeah, that's further than they can think. It has to get so bad to make Boise do anything, you know. Yeah. Because they just we kind of slide along. Well, we'd all rather go to the hills. That's right. He's heading to McCall for the summer. Maybe for the year. For the Year. You go to McCall? Yeah, we have a place. McCall and I work at the golf course. Oh. You do? Yes. My. My son works there, too. Every day at hitting the ball. No he doesn't. I want to tell you one last little story. That we had a neighbor named Mr. Johnson. And he had a company. A company? He had a little granddaughter and her name was Eleanor. And on top of the tower at the station. Yep. If you look at it, it looks like it's a little plate for tea. And then the little tiny, uh, what do you call them on top? And so every time she went by. They would in their family would say, it's Eleanor's teapot. Oh, it was known as Eleanor. That shape up at the top. Especially around the rim with. It's so cute. Yeah. The the pinnacle. Pinnacle. What's the word for pinnacle? Elmer Johnson was a great builder. A lot of the homes on Boise Avenue. He built those homes. He was just he was a good builder. Yeah. And a good fisherman, a good fisherman. Yeah, So thank you. Well, I'm thanking you all so much for doing this. Let me see if I can. Do you think you can get it? Oh, sorry. I raised it up. Let's see if I get out of your way. You can. They're here.

metadata.title: Introduction and Interview Setup
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer, Thad Webster, introduces himself, the oral history project, and the participants, Ramona and Adelia Garro. He explains the purpose of the interview, the context of the America 250 in Idaho commemoration, and the technical setup for recording. The participants are asked for permission to record and to spell their names, establishing the formal start of the oral history session.

metadata.title: First Memories of the Boise Depot
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer invites Ramona and Adelia to share their earliest memories of the Boise Depot. Adelia recalls visiting the depot as a child during World War II, describing the bustling scene filled with soldiers. Ramona reflects on the depot as a constant, beautiful presence in her childhood, mentioning the Platt Gardens and a memorable gazebo. Both women express a sense of awe and nostalgia for the depot's atmosphere and significance in their early lives.

lines.line: # Family Background and Neighborhood Life

> The conversation shifts to the sisters' upbringing, detailing their family's move to a small farm on Iowa Street in Boise. They recount how their father, a Basque barber, acquired the land and built their family home in 1935. The sisters share anecdotes about their home, including a hopscotch drawing in the basement, and discuss the evolution of their neighborhood, highlighting its rural character and the continuity of family ownership.

metadata.title: Family Background and Neighborhood Life
metadata.synopsis: The conversation shifts to the sisters' upbringing, detailing their family's move to a small farm on Iowa Street in Boise. They recount how their father, a Basque barber, acquired the land and built their family home in 1935. The sisters share anecdotes about their home, including a hopscotch drawing in the basement, and discuss the evolution of their neighborhood, highlighting its rural character and the continuity of family ownership.

lines.line: # Streetcars, Local Architecture, and Community History

> The discussion explores the history of the South Boise Village area, including its incorporation into Boise and the presence of streetcars that once connected the neighborhood to the city. The sisters reminisce about the remnants of streetcar tracks, distinctive local architecture such as the Dutch house, and notable businesses like Thompson's Appliance. They reflect on the changing landscape and the sense of community that characterized their childhood environment.

metadata.title: Streetcars, Local Architecture, and Community History
metadata.synopsis: The discussion explores the history of the South Boise Village area, including its incorporation into Boise and the presence of streetcars that once connected the neighborhood to the city. The sisters reminisce about the remnants of streetcar tracks, distinctive local architecture such as the Dutch house, and notable businesses like Thompson's Appliance. They reflect on the changing landscape and the sense of community that characterized their childhood environment.

metadata.title: Family Train Travel and Personal Experiences
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer asks about family trips involving the depot, prompting stories about relatives traveling by train. Adelia recalls taking her aunt to the depot and mentions her own journey to Portland with her uncle in fifth grade. The sisters discuss their limited experiences riding trains from the depot, the excitement of travel, and the connection between their family and the railroad through relatives who worked on the trains.

lines.line: # Train Whistles, Barber School, and Sheep Herding Stories

> The conversation turns to family anecdotes, including memories of hearing train whistles at night and stories about their father's brother attending barber school in Portland. The sisters share humorous and poignant tales from their father's days as a sheep herder, including his first encounter with a salmon and his reflections on the hardships of herding. These stories highlight the family's immigrant background and the challenges faced by earlier generations.

metadata.title: Train Whistles, Barber School, and Sheep Herding Stories
metadata.synopsis: The conversation turns to family anecdotes, including memories of hearing train whistles at night and stories about their father's brother attending barber school in Portland. The sisters share humorous and poignant tales from their father's days as a sheep herder, including his first encounter with a salmon and his reflections on the hardships of herding. These stories highlight the family's immigrant background and the challenges faced by earlier generations.

lines.line: # Train Accidents and Local Tragedies

> The interviewer brings up a train crash near Thanksgiving 1951, but the sisters do not recall hearing it. This leads to a discussion of a tragic accident involving their mother's friend, Miss Ruth Redmond, who was killed when her car stalled on the tracks. The sisters reflect on the rarity of such accidents and the emotional impact of losing someone in the community to a train-related incident.

metadata.title: Train Accidents and Local Tragedies
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer brings up a train crash near Thanksgiving 1951, but the sisters do not recall hearing it. This leads to a discussion of a tragic accident involving their mother's friend, Miss Ruth Redmond, who was killed when her car stalled on the tracks. The sisters reflect on the rarity of such accidents and the emotional impact of losing someone in the community to a train-related incident.

lines.line: # Depot Renovations and Community Reactions

> The topic shifts to memories of depot renovations, particularly those initiated by Bill Agee in the 1990s. The sisters recall community resistance to changes, the beauty of the restoration, and the folklore surrounding soldiers passing through Boise during wartime. They discuss the significance of the depot's location and its symbolic connection to the Capitol, as well as stories about notable Boise families like the Angletons.

metadata.title: Depot Renovations and Community Reactions
metadata.synopsis: The topic shifts to memories of depot renovations, particularly those initiated by Bill Agee in the 1990s. The sisters recall community resistance to changes, the beauty of the restoration, and the folklore surrounding soldiers passing through Boise during wartime. They discuss the significance of the depot's location and its symbolic connection to the Capitol, as well as stories about notable Boise families like the Angletons.

lines.line: # Closing Stories and Final Thanks

> As the interview concludes, the participants share a final anecdote about a neighbor's granddaughter and the distinctive architecture atop the depot, affectionately known as 'Eleanor's teapot.' They reflect on the neighbor's contributions to local homebuilding and fishing. The interviewer expresses gratitude for the sisters' participation, bringing the oral history session to a warm and appreciative close.

metadata.title: Closing Stories and Final Thanks
metadata.synopsis: As the interview concludes, the participants share a final anecdote about a neighbor's granddaughter and the distinctive architecture atop the depot, affectionately known as 'Eleanor's teapot.' They reflect on the neighbor's contributions to local homebuilding and fishing. The interviewer expresses gratitude for the sisters' participation, bringing the oral history session to a warm and appreciative close.

lines.line: # Depot Architecture, Union Pacific, and Sun Valley

> The interviewer and participants discuss the architectural grandeur of the Boise Depot, speculating on Union Pacific's motivations for building such an impressive structure in a residential area. They compare the Boise Depot to less ornate depots in other Idaho cities and consider the influence of Spanish style and local cultural factors. The conversation also touches on the development of Sun Valley by Union Pacific and the Harriman family's role in Idaho's history.

metadata.title: Depot Architecture, Union Pacific, and Sun Valley
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer and participants discuss the architectural grandeur of the Boise Depot, speculating on Union Pacific's motivations for building such an impressive structure in a residential area. They compare the Boise Depot to less ornate depots in other Idaho cities and consider the influence of Spanish style and local cultural factors. The conversation also touches on the development of Sun Valley by Union Pacific and the Harriman family's role in Idaho's history.

lines.line: # The Great Depression, Train Innovations, and Sun Valley

> The discussion delves into the context of the depot's construction during the Great Depression, noting the irony of such an investment during economic hardship. The interviewer explains how train companies sought to attract passengers through innovations like the streamliner and by building destination resorts such as Sun Valley. The participants share family stories about early skiing in Sun Valley and reflect on the ambitious projects undertaken by Union Pacific during this era.

metadata.title: The Great Depression, Train Innovations, and Sun Valley
metadata.synopsis: The discussion delves into the context of the depot's construction during the Great Depression, noting the irony of such an investment during economic hardship. The interviewer explains how train companies sought to attract passengers through innovations like the streamliner and by building destination resorts such as Sun Valley. The participants share family stories about early skiing in Sun Valley and reflect on the ambitious projects undertaken by Union Pacific during this era.

lines.line: # Ownership Changes and Amtrak Service

> The conversation covers the transition of the depot's ownership from Union Pacific to MK, and eventually to the city of Boise after MK's bankruptcy. The sisters express relief and gratitude for the city's acquisition and the preservation of the depot. The interviewer provides historical context about the end of passenger service in 1971, the arrival of Amtrak in 1977, and the various arrangements for depot use during these transitions.

metadata.title: Ownership Changes and Amtrak Service
metadata.synopsis: The conversation covers the transition of the depot's ownership from Union Pacific to MK, and eventually to the city of Boise after MK's bankruptcy. The sisters express relief and gratitude for the city's acquisition and the preservation of the depot. The interviewer provides historical context about the end of passenger service in 1971, the arrival of Amtrak in 1977, and the various arrangements for depot use during these transitions.

lines.line: # The Depot’s Role in the Community and Amtrak’s Departure

> The interviewer shares his experience as a depot tour guide and recounts the community's strong sense of ownership over the building. He describes the controversy over Amtrak's place in the depot during MK's ownership, the eventual compromise to house Amtrak in a separate section, and the end of Amtrak service in 1997. The sisters recall special train excursions and the public's emotional attachment to the depot as a civic landmark.

metadata.title: The Depot’s Role in the Community and Amtrak’s Departure
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer shares his experience as a depot tour guide and recounts the community's strong sense of ownership over the building. He describes the controversy over Amtrak's place in the depot during MK's ownership, the eventual compromise to house Amtrak in a separate section, and the end of Amtrak service in 1997. The sisters recall special train excursions and the public's emotional attachment to the depot as a civic landmark.

lines.line: # Missed Opportunities for Commuter Rail

> The interviewer raises the issue of increasing traffic congestion in the Treasure Valley and the lack of commuter rail service despite existing tracks. The sisters discuss past efforts to introduce commuter trains, including a demonstration project, and speculate on why such initiatives failed. They attribute the lack of progress to insufficient traffic problems at the time and Boise's slow approach to change, expressing hope that worsening congestion might eventually prompt action.

metadata.title: Missed Opportunities for Commuter Rail
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer raises the issue of increasing traffic congestion in the Treasure Valley and the lack of commuter rail service despite existing tracks. The sisters discuss past efforts to introduce commuter trains, including a demonstration project, and speculate on why such initiatives failed. They attribute the lack of progress to insufficient traffic problems at the time and Boise's slow approach to change, expressing hope that worsening congestion might eventually prompt action.

story.title: Boise Depot - Ramona Higer, Adelia Simplot 03/25/2026
story.description: Personal memories of growing up in South Boise and visiting the Boise Depot
